flashcards.front: Time Complexity: Insert at Beginning of Singly Linked List
flashcards.back:
O(1)

Just update two pointers: newNode->next = head; head = newNode;

flashcards.front: Time Complexity: Delete from End of Singly Linked List
flashcards.back:
O(n)

Must traverse to find the second-to-last node because singly linked lists do not have a backward pointer.

flashcards.front: Steps to Reverse a Singly Linked List Iteratively
flashcards.back:
1. Save next: next = curr->next
2. Reverse link: curr->next = prev
3. Move prev: prev = curr
4. Move curr: curr = next

Finally: head = prev

Use slow (1 step) and fast (2 steps) pointers.

If they meet: cycle exists.
If fast reaches NULL: no cycle.

Time: O(n), Space: O(1)

flashcards.front: Two Pointer Technique for Finding Middle Element
flashcards.back:
Slow moves 1 step, fast moves 2 steps.

When fast reaches end, slow is at the middle.

For even length, returns the second middle node.

flashcards.front: Singly vs Doubly Linked List Trade-off
flashcards.back:
Singly: Less memory (one pointer per node), can only traverse forward, delete from end is O(n).

Doubly: More memory (two pointers per node), bidirectional traversal, delete from end is O(1) with tail pointer.
